courses.name: Elementary Language Tutorial
courses.description: Elementary study of the language required for studying a religious tradition.

courses.name: Judaism, Christianity, Islam
courses.description: A survey of the history, beliefs and practices of these major religious traditions.

courses.name: Religions of South and East Asia
courses.description: A survey of the history, beliefs, and practices of South and East Asian religious traditions, including Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, Sikhism, Daoism, Confucianism, and Shinto.

courses.name: Religion and Culture
courses.description: Interpreting how religion is represented and expressed by great works of literature, film, art, music, and popular culture. Topics include myth and ritual, community and identity, body and sexuality, sacred space, creativity and imagination.

courses.name: Global Religions: Identity and Community
courses.description: An introduction to major issues in the study of religion in global contexts, drawing on historical and contemporary examples.

courses.name: Intermediate Language Tutorial
courses.description: Intermediate study of the language required for studying a religious tradition. Restricted to students registered in a Religion program.

courses.name: Judaism
courses.description: The history of Judaism and the Jewish people from the Second Temple until the present day. The organization, basic beliefs, social and ethical practices of the Jews and Judaism.

courses.name: Hebrew Bible
courses.description: An introduction to the foundational text for Abrahamic religions that places its various literary genres, theologies, myths and histories within a larger ancient Near Eastern context. All texts in English translation.

courses.name: Christianity
courses.description: An introduction to the history, beliefs, traditions, practices, and diversity of Christianity from its beginnings to the present day.

courses.name: Early Christianity
courses.description: Introduction to the critical study of the writings of the New Testament with discussion of their Hellenistic and Jewish background, the historical Jesus, Paul and his letters, and historical and sociological explanations for the rise of the early church and interpretation of its writings.

courses.name: Global Christianity
courses.description: Survey of recent and current Christian movements around the world, both by region and thematically, with emphasis on institutions and networks that connect Christian communities across national boundaries. Special consideration is given to the cultural and political capacities of such Christian communities and networks.

courses.name: Islam
courses.description: The study of Muslim religious tradition and investigation of its organization, basic beliefs, social and ethical principles and practices.

courses.name: The Qur’an
courses.description: An examination of the Qur'an's content, form, style, central themes, canonization, and classical and contemporary interpretive traditions.  All texts are in English.

courses.name: Buddhism
courses.description: Basic beliefs and practices of the Buddhist tradition and a brief survey of its development and transformations in India, Sri Lanka, Southeast Asia, Tibet, China and Japan.

courses.name: Hinduism
courses.description: Basic beliefs, practices, and social structures of the Hindu tradition as reflected in Hindu scriptures, myths and symbols, and philosophical schools.

courses.name: Religion and Gender
courses.description: An exploration of issues related to gender and religion in historical and contemporary contexts.

courses.name: Religions of China
courses.description: Survey of the origins, development, and diffusion of Chinese religious traditions, including Confucianism, Daoism, Chinese Buddhism and popular religion(s).

courses.name: Maccabees to Muhammad
courses.description: The early history, literature and ideas of Judaism, Christianity and Islam from 200 BCE to 750 CE.

courses.name: Love and Its Myths
courses.description: An exploration of love as expressed in religious literatures and religious practices.

courses.name: Religious Diversity of Canada
courses.description: An historical survey emphasizing the interactions of various religious traditions in Canada, including indigenous religions, Christian missionary and colonial traditions, immigrant and global diaspora religions.

courses.name: Mystical and Contemplative Traditions
courses.description: An historical and functional study of mystical experiences in their religious contexts, relying on examples from selected traditions such as the Christian, Buddhist, Hindu, Jewish and Muslim.

courses.name: Indigenous Religions of Canada
courses.description: Religions of Inuit, First Nations and Métis peoples, past and present. Considerations include concepts of tradition, syncretism and “creative ritual.” Primary sources may include textual, visual and oral materials. Course may include fieldwork, as well as in-class presentations by community elders.

courses.name: Death and Afterlife
courses.description: The meaning of death and afterlife in some religious traditions and secular philosophies with emphasis on the Hindu teaching of the immortal soul; the Hebraic idea of collective survival; the Christian doctrine of resurrection of the body; the Buddhist conception of no-soul and nirvana.

courses.name: Greek Religion
courses.description: A study of religion in ancient Greece.

courses.name: Religion and Society
courses.description: Cross-cultural survey of religious institutions, focusing on theories and methodologies in the study of religion. Topics may include myth, totemism, cults, ritual, belief systems, altered states of consciousness, new religious and/or new age movements and the relationship of religion with other social institutions and processes.

courses.name: Roman Religion
courses.description: A study of religion in ancient Rome.

courses.name: Philosophy of Religion
courses.description: A study of philosophical issues arising from religion. Topics may include: arguments for and against the existence of God, religious experience, death and the afterlife, miracles, God and evil, the relationship between religion and science, and the relationship between religion and ethics.

courses.name: Big Questions in Religious Studies
courses.description: In this Inquiry course, students will be introduced to a specific topic in Religious Studies (e.g., ritual, narrative, space) and develop a research project related to it. Focus on fostering intellectual curiosity and developing practical skills of reading, writing and research fundamentals.

courses.name: Indigenous Traditions
courses.description: This course illuminates a recent category of “World Religions” by examining cases from all five continents, as well as in diaspora (e.g., Brazilian Candomblé, Roma/Sinti religion). Considerations include the study of minority religions, religion in oral cultures, myth & ritual studies, colonialism, globalization.

courses.name: Special Topics in Religion and Popular Culture
courses.description: Examination of interactions between religion and popular culture in the form of music, film, video games, literature, and other media. Topic and focus will vary year to year; please check departmental website for information. May be repeated for credit when the topic changes.

courses.name: Religions and the Environment
courses.description: Attitudes in the major world religions to nature and the environment and recent responses by religious traditions to ecological degradation and crisis. Includes examination of religious sensibilities expressed in environmentalism.

courses.name: Topics in Religion
courses.description: Content of this course may vary from year to year.  Please check departmental website for information on the topic.

courses.name: Religion and Public Life
courses.description: This course examines some aspects of the intersection between religion(s) and public life, broadly construed, including social, economic, political, institutional aspects, either in the contemporary world or focused on a particular historical period.

courses.name: Advanced Language Tutorial
courses.description: Advanced study of the language required for studying a religious tradition.

courses.name: Special Topics in Religions and the Body
courses.description: Discussion of the embodiment of religious ideas in life, law, and practice, for example in food consumption, gender ideologies, sexuality, adornment, and death rituals. Topic will vary year to year; please check departmental website for information. May be repeated for credit when the topic changes.

courses.name: The Holocaust: Historical and Religious Dimensions
courses.description: Introduction to the historical and religious dimensions of the Holocaust.  The foundations, perpetration and consequences of the Nazi Final Solution through primary sources including survivor testimony will be examined.

courses.name: Antisemitism, Then and Now
courses.description: An examination of the long history of antisemitism to understand how historical forms of antisemitism have endured into the present and evolved over time. A variety of texts, images, media representations, and oral histories will be explored using methodologies from history and religious studies.

courses.name: Reformation Europe
courses.description: A history of the Protestant and Catholic Reformations of the sixteenth century, with emphasis on the theological disputes of the protagonists and the impact of these disputes on the social, political and cultural developments of the era.

courses.name: Jesus of Nazareth
courses.description: A study of the historical records of the life of Jesus, the methods used to interpret them, and the resulting images of Jesus.

courses.name: Paul of Tarsus
courses.description: The social, religious, and historical context of Paul, the communities he founded, and the letters he wrote to them.

courses.name: Christian Discipline
courses.description: An historical survey of key Christian thought and practices at the individual and collective level.  Topics may include self-discipline, body discipline, monastic discipline, church discipline and social discipline.

courses.name: Evangelical Christianity in Social-Historical Perspective
courses.description: The development of some protestant Christianities in relation to material factors, such as colonialism, industrial or consumer capitalism, imperialism, and in relation to major ideological trends, such as nationalism, economic or political liberalism and atheism.

courses.name: Music, Religion, and Spiritual Practices
courses.description: Through various case studies, this course considers the role music plays in selected religions and spiritual practices.

courses.name: Sufism
courses.description: An introduction to the main practical and theoretical dimensions of Islam's mystical tradition as seen through the life and work of its key representatives.

courses.name: Topics in Magic, Witchcraft, and the Occult
courses.description: Studies in issues related to magic, witchcraft and/or the occult in various historical, religious, and cultural contexts.

courses.name: The Life and Image of Muhammad
courses.description: Overview of the life and teaching of the Prophet Muhammad, and the most salient motifs and features of Muslim devotion to him.

courses.name: Special Topics in Islamic Texts & Narratives
courses.description: A focus on post-Qur'anic Islamic literature and interpretive traditions (e.g. tafsir, hadith); texts and topics will vary from year to year;  please check departmental website for information. May be repeated for credit when the topic changes.

courses.name: Early Buddhism
courses.description: An exploration of the development of early Buddhist philosophy, psychology, religious texts, and practices.

courses.name: Buddhism Beyond India
courses.description: This course explores a variety of topics associated with the development and transmission of Mahayana Buddhism throughout Asia, including scripture and narrative, ritual and practice, and art and architecture.

courses.name: Early Hinduism
courses.description: A historical survey of Hinduism from the Vedic era to the development of devotional Hinduism.  Vedic religion and developments in early Hindu Philosophy and sectarian Hinduism.

courses.name: Modern Hinduism
courses.description: A survey of major developments in Hinduism since the period of colonial British rule.  The development of “reform” Hinduism in the 18th and 19th centuries, and the emergence of Hindu nationalist movements in the 20th century.

courses.name: Religion and Violence
courses.description: This course examines “religious violence” from past and present, from the large (state violence) to the small (self-harm), and in between (communal violence, intimate partner violence), and asks why only some violence is commonly deemed religious and only some religion is commonly deemed violent.

courses.name: Studies in Greek Art
courses.description: A study of period or theme in the art and archaeology of Ancient Greece. Topics may vary from year to year.

courses.name: Studies in Roman Art
courses.description: A study of a period or theme in the art and archaeology of the ancient Romans. Topics may vary from year to year.

courses.name: Classical Approaches to Religion
courses.description: Examination of reflection on the nature and origin of religion from the ancient world up to key figures and founders of the discipline of the systematic, critical, and scientific study of religion in the nineteenth and early twentieth century.

courses.name: Special Topics in Religion
courses.description: Content of this course may vary from year to year.  Please check departmental website for information on the topic.

courses.name: Topics in the Study of Religion Abroad
courses.description: This travel course explores religion in its historical and/or contemporary contexts in a particular geographic locale.  Travel destinations, religious traditions studied, course content, and themes vary from year to year.

courses.name: Is Religious Freedom a Human Right?
courses.description: Legal, theoretical, and theological interconnections between religion and human rights. Evaluation of concepts including religious freedom, secularism, public sphere, accommodation and neutrality. Examination of religion and culture, interdependence of legal and religious perspectives, boundaries of religion and state, and religious compulsion. Use of case studies.

courses.name: Contemporary Issues in the Study of Religion
courses.description: This course engages with the real world implications of late twentieth and twenty-first century scholarship on religion with a focus on applied learning and developing employable skills that facilitate transition from academia to a career. Highly recommended for students considering graduate school.

courses.name: Tutorial
courses.description: A tutorial on a topic in religious studies. Contents of the tutorial to be arranged with the supervising faculty member.

courses.name: Seminar in the Study of Religion
courses.description: Content of this course may vary from year to year.  Please consult the departmental website for information on the topic.

courses.name: Religion and Public Life: Community-Engaged Learning
courses.description: Critical reflection on the theme of religion and public life as evidenced in today's Ottawa. Experiential learning via a 30-hour placement, contextualized through readings and in-class sessions (in weeks 3, 6, 12), and culminating in a reflective final project.

courses.name: Honours Research Essay
courses.description: Honours research paper (approx. 40 pages) is due on the last day of winter term classes. Written proposal due to the Proposal Board on the first day of fall term classes.  Please consult department document for full requirements and information.

free_electives.text: 7.  4.0 credits in free electives (can be in RELI) 4.0
7. Sufficient free electives to make 20.0 credits for the degree
6.  3.0 credits in free electives (can be in RELI) 3.0

free_electives.degree: Specialization in Global Religions: Identity and Community B.G.In.S. Honours (20.0 credits)
Stream in Global Religions: Identity and Community B.G.In.S. (15.0 credits)
